replies.text: +Bob Wilson A modern laser jet will print down to 20 microns or so with particles 5 microns or so across - so i would guess this can be done - you planning on trying?

replies.author: @johnwilson3668
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith 


In time, as it is quite complicated . Need to finish refurbishing my lab firstThe platters used in hard drive disk's are coated in a thin film of 
ferromagnetic material . Using a read-and-write head and
stepper motors driven in opposing axis magnetically print  continuous
lines to a high definition . I conferred with a scientist friend of mine
who is from the university where graphine was invented .He also
told me graphine is easily obtainable 3 micron across . when I said print, I meant electronically print
a continues line instead of writing data using a read and write head .

author: @Allwestconstruction
text:
Thinking outside the box...........well, maybe thinking outside my box- Question for you-  since a capacitor is charged by separating the protons and electrons and storing them on two separate plates, would it be possible  to  somehow charge a capacitor with magnets 
Maybe connect the two plates and apply magnets, one side of magnet negative attracting positive, and the other magnet positive attracting negative, before you remove magnets, disconnect plates 

I have already done some research and found nothing, so I thought I would run this by you to see if my thinking was way outside the box, maybe at the least, having magnets in place when charging may help charging?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Steve Allwest in my understanding mate capacitors store energy in an electric field - charge separation is a methodology by which this field is built and maintained - there is a relationship between magnetic and electric fields but it's not straight forward - i think it is an interesting investigation but i also think a static magnetic field with a static electric field won't help - the relationship seems to involve movement - think about current generation - you have to move the magnet. Anyway, very interesting question - I hope you pursue it

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith  Thanks mate, this fun thought of trying to get power from two standing magnets  needs a lot more research before I spend the time trying it, thanks for responding, Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Steve Allwest let me know how you get on mate

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction

If you had a mind to-
1. Apply magnets to one of your deep eutectic fluids in a glass jar , Negative face on one side of the jar and positive face on  the other side, and let stand overnight to see if you get any separation

author: @gristlevonraben
text: please forgive me, I'm new to all this.  I saw your video on making graphene sheets by a friction process, making the top of the graphene dusted sheet thinner and thinner by stripping the layers down until they are nano-thin.  The friction process used orbital motion, but if you could strip the top layers off in straight lines, you could cause the particles to align better.  I'm gonna order some supplies and work on it.  I think, though, it might be easier to actually get the graphene to stick to the material by using its static charge.  it would be like a tv screen attracting dust, but the dust would be graphene.  That doesn't make it run in straight lines, but after the thin layer is on there, i could work on a way to polish it in straight lines.  I'd have to see what could make trillions of small valleys cheaply, it'd have to be finer than the finest brushes.  Your friction process creates the heat necessary to cause the plastic to help bond the carbon to the plastic.

replies.author: @gristlevonraben
replies.text: They use static to make sand paper, instead of sand dust, imagine carbon dust.  lastly, to make the particles align, before the glue has set, blow a jet of air through an extremely small slit at an angle over the sheet while it is rolling in the conveyer belt.  I will work on how to induce directional movement of particles in a solution for your ink graphene sheets.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Gristle Von Raben looking forward to what you come up with mate - nice ideas

author: @DanielbenDaniel
text:
I had thoughts about posting this a few months back, simply forgot. better late than never.  
  A few months ago I was playing with some graphite, after 3 days in a ball mill. One batch with marbles & one batch with metal ball bearings. We sonicated some ball bearing compound into water (well water). After a day or 2 it settled out & we poured off the top layers to test out. Then took the remainder settlements and shook it up. Long story short. Was looking into the mix with a phone flash light. We noticed the light looked like it was moving some of the suspended particles We where like "Wow cool" but then we got out the Neo Magnets and WOW. I believe the iron in our well water & maybe the metal bearings them self made the graphite/graphene mix magnetizable. I also notice that the faster you moved the magnets the more pronounced the movement was. My first thoughts was a liquid generator could be possible.
  The glass marble batch slightly repelled under the effect of magnetism.  Not nearly as mush movement but noticeable repelling. 
  I think that painting graphene type products in a high magnetic flux, much like flip flop multi-colored paints, one could influence the patterns of the particles lays.  A mixture of magnetized & non magnetizable graphene might be able to influence lays as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Now that is interesting - i suspect the grinding media had a bigger effect mate - it could be the iron in the well water or small amounts of grinding media 'rubbed' off on the graphite - either way it is a very interesting effect. Mixing the two 50:50 , painting a sheet and drying it in a magnetic field could have an interesting effect especially if one was hydrophobic and one was hydrophilic

author: @noworldorderforme
text: Hi Robert. You may want to re-think your position re solenoids. Solenoids do not produce an even field of magnetic flux. Have a quick look at this video: PH EM MF DEMO 70001A V1025 2D Magnetic Field Demonstrations Simple Wire Coils which shows the magnetic field around three different types of solenoid. The third one, where several coils are aligned in a row produces an aligned field which if applied around the ink whilst it is drying might produce a much finer alignment of the graphene. There may be a certain amount of 'tuning' required vis a vis turns of wire etc, but you ought to be able to produce much better results using this method. Apologies in advance if I'm being dim, but it looks like it might be worth a try.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for that mate - it looks really good - everyone needs their eyes opening now and then lol

author: @nitelite78
text:
Layman here. Would having more lines make it better e.g say if you could print them? Is it correct that you would still need the magnets to align the individual particles in each line? Is it that you need to do the alignment when the ink is wet and is it the drying of the ink that fixes the alignment?

Have I understood what is happening there?

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
you seem on the right path ... if still wet, they are movable so flux aligns them with max conductivity ... more lines = thinner lines -> more resistance ... fine balance act between distance from anode to cathode and specific resistance ... a bit like the copper conductance ... how long vs how tick :) resistance is inverse proportional to thickness and directly proportional to distance. :)
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrical_resistance_and_conductance

author: @actudoran
text:
excellent video as usual.
nice talk & demo of the mag arrangement.
I get it ... no magnetic field to be placed around the cell ... cool.
still ... consider that antennae arrangement whereby interference like patterns in 3d can be printed onto PET with dried inks ... i'll try to sketch it up for you to see whether we are on the same page :) 
BTW since the flux is unidirectional ...I get the diode thing ;)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cool - i think your idea of magnets is a good one mate just for different implementations like antennas

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
Yup ... perm magnets and antennae seem a logical combo but ... anions and cathions travel along a path and can be perhaps encouraged to do so quicker :) 
Mind you , when discussing already a two dimensional plane like the pet painted with low resistance already vectored paint ... not much point for further encouragement ... however in a 3d context with lots of graphene floating in an electrolyte ... ;)  
Might even be a point to make in a supercapacitor design due to the orientation of the graphene "flakes" along the lines formed by mag field.  Much like lines up hedgehogs .... hehe

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
*****
interesting thought mate - thanks for that
